slides.title: Turning TS Off 📴
slides.content:
Sometimes you truly don't know what type a variable will be, or you're pulling in data from a weird third-party library.

TypeScript provides an "escape hatch" called the `any` type.

slides.title: Whatever You Want
slides.content: A variable typed as `any` acts exactly like a plain JavaScript variable:

quizData.question: What happens if you type a variable as `any`?

options.text: It restricts the variable to only accept numbers or strings.
It essentially turns off TypeScript checking for that variable.
It throws an immediate compilation error.

quizData.explanation: The `any` type allows a variable to hold any kind of data without triggering type errors.

slides.title: Use Sparingly! ⚠️
slides.content:
While `any` might seem convenient if you don't feel like typing something properly, **it defeats the entire purpose of TypeScript!**

Using `any` removes all the safety nets. You won't get Intellisense autocomplete, and the compiler won't warn you if you call a non-existent function on it.

slides.title: The Better 'Any' 💡
slides.content: TypeScript offers a safer version of `any` called `unknown` (which we will cover in Advanced lessons). If you genuinely don't know a type, try to use `unknown` instead!
